---
title: "final_visualizations"
author: Henry Oliver
date: March 9, 2026
format: html
warning: False
code-fold: true
---


1. Summarize the key feedback points you received on your FPM #3 draft visualizations.

My main feedback on my map images was to find a way to label my countries, and to change my line colors in my trend graph to correlate with the colors. I was also advised to zero in on specific countries on my trend so it wasn't so crowded. And on one of my figures that I didn't end up using, but did do a similar trend, I was advised to put more text explaining what's going on, or just make it more explicit what relatively complex figures are showing. In my waffle chart, I was both advised to put units in, and to keep units out of it, because the visual is what's important.

2. Explain which of these suggestions you plan to implement and how.

I want to get less lines on my trend graphs. I've determined that six lines on each graph is the perfect number so things don't get too crowded. I'm going to pick countries that have wide spread of demographic factors and are spread out in the region. I've decided to go with Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Iran, Egypt, Kuwait, Mauritania. I also plan on removing units from my trend plots so that people focus on the trends - this should remove some of the complications. I'm already going to need a lot of text, so I don't want too much on my figures if I can avoid it. I also want my trend lines to correspond with the color of the respective country on the map so I don't have to label countries on my map - I intend on doing this in affinity. I was also advised to make it more clear why my initial waffle chart was just Iran - I intend on making a waffle chart that is for the whole region. I'm going to do this by summing withdrawals and available resources for all countries, and making my chart from this instead of just Iranian data.

3. Identify any areas where you decided not to follow feedback and explain why (e.g. “it didn’t align with my main message” or “I found a different design solution”).

As discussed earlier I opted not to put more text in my actual figures - I am adding captions but I don't want to explain too much about my metrics. I want people to focus on the trends. One of my peers recommended that I find a way to have trends appear on my map, rather than just having a map for 2000 and 2020. Because I don't want my plot to just be bunch of maps, I'm going to stick with my trend graph.

4. Address which (if any) of the ten design elements you do not plan to implement (remember, not all ten will necessarily apply to your project, and that’s okay!).

10) applying a DEI lens to your design, as appropriate (e.g. considering the people / communities / places represented in your data, consider how you frame your questions / issue)

I'm not making much of an effort to bring historical or cultural context to my display. I want to make sure that my analysis doesn't come across as critical or negative - but I am highlighting a sustainability concern. I plan on avoiding terms like "water stress" and trying to avoid viewer conclusion that people in this area are "using too much water". I think I can do this by having a warm tone in my final graphic, and addressing the affects of regional climate and climate change.



```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("eds230_final_viz.png")
```



## Libraries
```{r}
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(here)
library(waffle)
library(sf)
library(ggspatial)
library(ggrepel)
})

my_tan <- "#CFA56A" # Save background color
```

## Read in Aquastat Data
```{r}
water <- read_csv(here("data/aquastat_nena.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>% 
  clean_names() 
  
```

## Clean Aquastat Data
```{r}
water_wider <- water %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = value,
              id_cols = c(area, year)) %>% 
  clean_names()
```

## Read in Map Data
```{r}
#| message: False
world <- st_read(here("data/countries/ne_50m_admin_0_countries.shp"), quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  clean_names()

# Filter and select
nena_region <- world %>%
  filter(continent %in% c("Asia", "Africa")) %>% 
  select(admin, geometry)
```

## Clean and Join Map Data
```{r}

water_wider$area[water_wider$area == "Iran (Islamic Republic of)"] <- "Iran"
water_wider$area[water_wider$area == "Syrian Arab Republic"] <- "Syria"
unique(water_wider$area)

common_names <- intersect(water_wider$area, nena_region$admin)

# Join geometry from nena_region into water_wider
water_nena <- water_wider %>%
  left_join(nena_region %>% select(admin, geometry), 
            by = c("area" = "admin")) %>% 
  st_as_sf() # Ensures R recognizes it as a spatial object

water_nena <- water_nena[water_nena$area != "Sudan", ]
```

### Map of NENA Area
```{r}
ref_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = world, fill = "white", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_sf(data = water_nena,  fill = "#388AA6", color = "grey20", linewidth = 0.6) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-22.34375, 74.53125), ylim = c(2.52476, 59.71210)) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = my_tan),
    # panel.border     = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 2)
    
  ) +
  annotation_north_arrow(
    location = "br", 
    which_north = "true",
    pad_x = unit(2, "cm"),
    style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering()) +
  annotation_scale()

ref_map
```

## Map for agriculture 2000
```{r}

ag_2000 <-
  # Filter to 2000
  ggplot(water_nena %>% filter(year=="2000")) +
  # Color by agricultural withdrawals
  geom_sf(aes(fill = agricultural_water_withdrawal_per_capita), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = 1, limits = c(0, 2250),
                       guide = guide_colorbar(direction = "vertical", title.position = "top")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "bl", which_north = "true", # North arrow
                         pad_x = unit(4.5, "cm"),
                         style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering()) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    legend.position = "left",
    legend.key.height = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "white", size = 24)
  ) +
  labs(fill = "Agricultural Water\nWithdrawal Per Capita", title = "2000")

ag_2000

```

## Map for agriculture 2020
```{r}

# Identical to previous except where commented
ag_2020 <- ggplot(water_nena %>% filter(year=="2020") %>% #filter to 2020
                    filter(area != "Sudan")) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = agricultural_water_withdrawal_per_capita), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "mako", direction = 1, limits = c(0, 2250),
                       guide = guide_colorbar(direction = "vertical", title.position = "top")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "bl", which_north = "true",
                         pad_x = unit(4.5, "cm"),
                         style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering()) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    legend.position = "right", # Put legend on opposite side
    legend.key.height = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "white", size = 24)
  ) +
  labs(fill = "Agricultural Water\nWithdrawal Per Capita", title = "2020")

ag_2020

```

## Agriculture Trend 2000-2020
```{r}


# Create label data for the last year each country has data
label_data <- water_nena %>%
  filter(area %in% c("Saudi Arabia", "Iraq", "Iran", "Egypt", "Kuwait", "Mauritania")) %>%
  filter(year >= 2000 & year <= 2022) %>%
  group_by(area) %>%
  filter(year == max(year))

ag_trend <- water_nena %>%
  filter(area %in% c("Saudi Arabia", "Iraq", "Iran", "Egypt", "Kuwait", "Mauritania")) %>%
  filter(year >= 2000 & year <= 2022) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = agricultural_water_withdrawal_per_capita, color = area)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 2) + # adjust line thickness
  geom_label_repel(
    data = label_data,
    aes(label = area),
    nudge_x = 1,
    direction = "y",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 4,
    fill = my_tan,
    label.size = NA  # removes border
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "mako") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.1, 0.1))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "white", size = 14),
    axis.ticks.x = element_line(color = "white"),
    axis.line.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL)

ag_trend
```

## Map for Water Stress 2000
```{r}

#  Prepare data and calculate the log10 ratio
map_data <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == "2000") %>%
  filter(area != "Northern Africa") %>% # Filter out continent level data
  filter(area != "Sudan") %>% # Filter out countries that don't have data in 2020
  mutate(
    ratio = total_water_withdrawal / total_renewable_water_resources,
    # log10(1) = 0. Values > 0 are over-withdrawing.
    log_ratio = log10(ratio) 
  ) %>%
  # Remove non-finite values (like log(0)) to avoid legend errors
  filter(is.finite(log_ratio))

# Capture the exact min and max for the legend
val_range <- range(map_data$log_ratio, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create logical breaks from min to max
log_breaks <- seq(floor(val_range[1] * 2) / 2, 
                      ceiling(val_range[2] * 2) / 2, 
                      by = 0.5)

# Map
stress_2000_log <- ggplot(map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = log_ratio), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "mako", 
    direction = 1,
    limits = val_range,       # Legend runs exactly from min to max
    breaks = log_breaks,  # Logical steps (e.g., -1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1)
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title =("Withdrawal/\nAvailable Resources\nlog scale"), 
      direction = "vertical", 
      title.position = "top",
      barheight = unit(3, "cm")
    )
  ) +
  annotation_north_arrow(
    location = "bl", 
    which_north = "true",
    pad_x = unit(4.5, "cm"),
    style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering()
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "white", size = 24)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "2000"
  )

stress_2000_log
```

## Map for Water Stress 2020
```{r}
#  Prepare data and calculate the log10 ratio
map_data <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == "2020") %>%
  filter(area != "Northern Africa") %>% # Filter out continent level data
  mutate(
    ratio = total_water_withdrawal / total_renewable_water_resources,
    # log10(1) = 0. Values > 0 are over-withdrawing.
    log_ratio = log10(ratio) 
  ) %>%
  # Remove non-finite values (like log(0)) to avoid legend errors
  filter(is.finite(log_ratio))

# Capture the exact min and max for the legend
val_range <- range(map_data$log_ratio, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create logical breaks from min to max
log_breaks <- seq(floor(val_range[1] * 2) / 2, 
                      ceiling(val_range[2] * 2) / 2, 
                      by = 0.5)

# 4. Map it
stress_2020_log <- ggplot(map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = log_ratio), color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "mako", 
    direction = 1,
    limits = val_range,       # Legend runs exactly from min to max
    breaks = logical_breaks,  # Logical steps (e.g., -1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1)
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title = element_blank(), 
      direction = "vertical", 
      title.position = "top",
      barheight = unit(3, "cm")
    )
  ) +
  annotation_north_arrow(
    location = "bl", 
    which_north = "true",
    pad_x = unit(4.5, "cm"),
    style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering()
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    legend.position = "right",
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "white", size = 24)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "2020"
  )

stress_2020_log
```

## Water Stress Trends 2000-2020
```{r}
# Create data labels
label_data <- water_nena %>%
  filter(area %in% c("Saudi Arabia", "Iraq", "Iran", "Egypt", "Kuwait", "Mauritania")) %>%
  filter(year >= 2000 & year <= 2020) %>% # Redundant filter for safety
  # Calculate ratio
  mutate(log_ratio = log10(total_water_withdrawal / total_renewable_water_resources)) %>%
  # Group countries
  group_by(area) %>%
  filter(year == max(year))

log_trend <- water_nena %>%
  filter(area %in% c("Saudi Arabia", "Iraq", "Iran", "Egypt", "Kuwait", "Mauritania")) %>%
  filter(year >= 2000 & year <= 2020) %>%
  mutate(log_ratio = log10(total_water_withdrawal / total_renewable_water_resources)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = log_ratio, color = area)) +
  # Add line where ration = 0 (Where withdrawals = resources)
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "white", linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 2) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = label_data,
    aes(label = area),
    nudge_x = 1,
    direction = "y",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 4,
    fill = my_tan,
    label.size = NA
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "mako") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.1, 0.1)),
    breaks = seq(2000, 2020, by = 5),
    labels = c("2000", "2005", "2010", "2015", "2020")
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + # Very minimal theme elements
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "white", size = 14),
    axis.ticks.x = element_line(color = "white"),
    axis.line.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL)

log_trend
```

## Waffle Charts for Resources vs Withdrawals
```{r}

library(patchwork)

# Save number of gallons in available resources
res_val <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == 2020) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(total_renewable_water_resources, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total) %>%
  .[1]

# Ag value
ag_val <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == 2020) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(agricultural_water_withdrawal, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total) %>%
  .[1]

# Municipal value
mun_val <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == 2020) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(municipal_water_withdrawal, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total) %>%
  .[1]

# Industrial value
ind_val <- water_nena %>%
  filter(year == 2020) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(industrial_water_withdrawal, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pull(total) %>%
  .[1]

# Waffle chart for total available resources
# Total withdrawals are 370 billion cubic meters - 1 square should equal 2 billion cubic meters
resources_waffle <- ggplot(data.frame(values = round(res_val/2),
                                      label = "Resources"),
       aes(fill = label, values = values)) +
  geom_waffle(n_rows = 10, color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Resources" = "#2a788e")) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",
  plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA)
)

# Waffle chart for withdrawals by sector. Total is 443 billion cubic meters - same square size
withdrawals_waffle <- tibble(
  label = c("Agricultural", "Municipal", "Industrial"),
  values = c(round(ag_val/2), round(mun_val/2), round(ind_val/2))
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(fill = label, values = values)) +
  geom_waffle(n_rows = 10, color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Agricultural" = "#22a884", "Municipal" = "#388AA6", "Industrial" = "grey60")) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "right") +
  theme(
  legend.position = "right",
  plot.background = element_rect(fill = my_tan, color = NA)
)

resources_waffle + withdrawals_waffle


```
